class_schedule.topic: The Global Digital Divide in Education
learning_objectives: Analyze the dimensions of the global digital divide: access, skills, and usage
Evaluate the impact of infrastructure gaps on educational opportunity
Compare national ed tech strategies across developing and developed nations
readings: Warschauer, M. - Technology and Social Inclusion Ch. 1-3
UNESCO - Global Education Monitoring Report (technology chapter)

lecture_notes.introduction: Globally, 2.7 billion people lack internet access. The digital divide is not just about devices — it encompasses infrastructure, electricity, digital skills, locally relevant content, and the cultural context of technology use.
key_concepts: Three levels of digital divide: access, skills, outcomes
Infrastructure: electricity, connectivity, device availability
Content gap: most educational content is in English and designed for Western contexts
Gender digital divide: women in LMICs are 20% less likely to use the internet
National ICT in education strategies: what works and what does not
lecture_notes.demonstration: Country comparison exercise: compare the ed tech strategies of two countries (one high-income, one low-income) using UNESCO data and identify structural advantages and disadvantages

review.value: First level: access to hardware and connectivity. Second level: skills to use technology effectively. Third level: ability to translate technology use into tangible social, economic, and educational outcomes.
Less than 5% of the world's languages are represented online. Students learning in local languages may find almost no digital educational content in their mother tongue.

class_schedule.topic: Ed Tech in Sub-Saharan Africa: Innovation Under Constraint
learning_objectives: Analyze successful educational technology initiatives in Sub-Saharan Africa
Design for low-resource environments: low bandwidth, feature phones, limited electricity
Evaluate the role of mobile technology as the primary computing platform in Africa
readings: Trucano, M. - Worst Practice in ICT Use in Education (World Bank blog series)
Aker, J. & Mbiti, I. - Mobile Phones and Economic Development in Africa

lecture_notes.introduction: Africa is not a technology desert. It is a laboratory for educational innovation under constraint. Some of the world's most creative ed tech solutions emerge from necessity: M-Pesa, Eneza Education, Ubongo, and Kolibri.
key_concepts: Mobile-first continent: smartphone penetration growing rapidly, feature phones still dominant
M-Shule and Eneza: SMS-based tutoring reaching millions
Ubongo: educational media reaching 17 million children across East Africa
Kolibri: offline-first learning platform for schools without internet
Solar-powered computing labs: BRCK, One Laptop Per Child lessons learned
lecture_notes.demonstration: Low-resource design challenge: design an educational technology solution for a rural Kenyan school with 2 hours of electricity per day, no internet, and shared feature phones among students
setup_requirements: Case studies of African ed tech initiatives

review.value: OLPC taught that hardware alone does not improve education. Without teacher training, curriculum integration, maintenance plans, and community buy-in, devices become expensive paperweights.

Learning Equality's Kolibri provides a full educational content library on a local server with no internet needed. Schools access Khan Academy, Wikipedia, and more via local WiFi. A model for offline-first education.

class_schedule.topic: Ed Tech in South and Southeast Asia
learning_objectives: Analyze large-scale ed tech initiatives in India, Indonesia, and the Philippines
Evaluate the impact of COVID-19 on educational technology adoption in Asia
Examine the tension between scale and quality in Asian ed tech
readings: Muralidharan, K. et al. - Disrupting Education? Experimental Evidence on Technology-Aided Instruction in India

lecture_notes.introduction: South and Southeast Asia face the challenge of educating billions of students across diverse languages, geographies, and socioeconomic conditions. Technology is seen as a potential equalizer, but implementation at scale is enormously complex.
key_concepts: India: DIKSHA national platform, Byju's disruption, Mindspark adaptive learning
Indonesia: Ruangguru and the tutoring platform model
Philippines: DepEd Commons and COVID-19 emergency distance learning
Language diversity: designing for hundreds of regional languages
COVID-19 impact: accelerated adoption with widened equity gaps
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case analysis: examine the Mindspark RCT (Muralidharan et al.) showing significant learning gains from adaptive learning in Indian public schools. Discuss what made it effective and whether it can scale.

review.value: One of the most rigorous ed tech RCTs in a developing country. Students using Mindspark's adaptive software for 90 minutes daily gained the equivalent of 2x normal learning gains in math and Hindi. Effect was largest for the lowest-performing students.

COVID-19 forced 1.6 billion students online. In Asia, students with devices and connectivity continued learning; those without fell further behind. The pandemic simultaneously proved technology's potential and exposed its equity failures.

class_schedule.topic: International Development and Ed Tech: Donors, NGOs, and Policy
learning_objectives: Evaluate the role of international development organizations in educational technology
Critique common failures in donor-driven ed tech initiatives
Design sustainable ed tech programs that survive beyond donor funding
readings: World Bank - World Development Report 2018: Learning to Realize Education's Promise (technology sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: International development organizations spend billions on educational technology in low-income countries. The results are mixed at best. Understanding why requires examining the institutional incentives, cultural dynamics, and sustainability challenges of donor-funded technology initiatives.
key_concepts: Major players: World Bank, USAID, DFID, UNESCO, Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation
Common failure modes: technology push, pilot-itis, sustainability gap, ignoring local context
Toyama's Law: technology amplifies existing human capacity; it does not substitute for it
Sustainability: what happens when the grant ends?
Locally-led innovation vs. externally-imposed solutions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Failure analysis: examine three failed ed tech development projects, identify the root cause of each failure, and propose redesigns that address sustainability
setup_requirements: Case studies of failed ed tech development projects

review.value: Technology amplifies existing human intent and capacity. In a school with good teachers and leadership, technology accelerates learning. In a dysfunctional system, technology amplifies dysfunction.

The tendency to run small, well-funded pilots that show promising results but never scale. Pilots succeed because of extra attention and resources that cannot be sustained at scale. The development community has funded thousands of pilots and scaled very few.

class_schedule.topic: Cultural Context and Localization in Educational Technology
learning_objectives: Analyze how cultural values affect technology adoption and use in education
Apply localization principles beyond translation: cultural adaptation of content and pedagogy
readings: Henderson, L. - Instructional Design of Interactive Multimedia: A Cultural Critique

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational technology designed in Silicon Valley does not automatically work in rural India, urban Lagos, or suburban Tokyo. Culture shapes how people learn, interact with technology, relate to authority, and construct knowledge.
key_concepts: Hofstede's cultural dimensions applied to ed tech: individualism/collectivism, power distance, uncertainty avoidance
Localization vs. translation: changing language is not enough
Pedagogical culture: collaborative vs. competitive, teacher-centered vs. learner-centered
Visual culture: color meanings, imagery norms, reading direction
Participatory design with local communities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Localization exercise: take an American educational app and identify 10 elements that would need cultural adaptation for use in Japan, Nigeria, or Brazil. Propose specific changes.

review.value: In high power distance cultures, learners may be uncomfortable questioning a digital tutor or selecting their own learning path. Design must respect cultural norms around authority while gradually building learner agency.

Localization includes: adapting examples to local contexts, using culturally appropriate imagery, adjusting pedagogical approach to cultural learning norms, respecting religious and social sensitivities, and ensuring idiomatic rather than literal translation.

class_schedule.topic: Global Ed Tech Policy and the Sustainable Development Goals
learning_objectives: Connect educational technology to the UN Sustainable Development Goals
Evaluate national ed tech policies across different country contexts
Design ed tech interventions aligned with SDG4 targets
readings: UNESCO - Reimagining Our Futures Together: A New Social Contract for Education (technology chapter)
UN Sustainable Development Goal 4: Quality Education (targets and indicators)

lecture_notes.introduction: SDG4 commits the world to 'ensure inclusive and equitable quality education and promote lifelong learning opportunities for all' by 2030. Technology is both a tool for achieving this goal and a potential source of new inequities if poorly implemented.
key_concepts: SDG4 targets relevant to ed tech: 4.1 (learning outcomes), 4.4 (digital skills), 4.a (effective learning environments)
National ed tech policy frameworks: what to include and what to avoid
Technology for crisis education: conflict, disaster, pandemic response
Open Educational Resources as a global equity strategy
The future: AI, big data, and global learning platforms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Policy analysis exercise: compare the national ed tech policies of Finland, Rwanda, and Uruguay, identifying what each prioritizes and what each overlooks

review.value: Technology appears throughout SDG4 targets: as a tool for reaching marginalized learners, a subject (digital skills), and an infrastructure requirement (ICT-equipped schools). But technology is a means, not an end.

COVID-19, conflicts in Ukraine, Syria, and Yemen, and climate disasters all disrupt education. Technology (radio, mobile, low-bandwidth platforms) can maintain learning continuity but requires pre-positioned infrastructure and content.

quizzes.topic: Digital Divide and Regional Ed Tech Landscapes

questions.question: Toyama argues that 'technology amplifies existing human intent and capacity.' Using a specific example from Sub-Saharan Africa or South Asia, explain what this means and its implications for ed tech development projects.
questions.answer: Toyama's law means technology does not create change independently — it amplifies what is already there. Example: the One Laptop Per Child program distributed laptops to schools in developing countries but saw minimal learning improvement because the underlying challenges (untrained teachers, lack of curriculum integration, absent school leadership) were not addressed. The laptops amplified the existing state: in schools with capable teachers, they were useful; in schools without support, they gathered dust. Implication for development: invest in teacher training, school leadership, and curriculum development BEFORE or simultaneously with technology deployment. Technology investment without human capacity investment wastes resources.

rubric.key: Draws clear implications for ed tech development

questions.question: What is the 'third level' of the digital divide, and why is it the most important for educational equity? Give an example.
questions.answer: The third level of the digital divide concerns the ability to translate technology access and skills into tangible outcomes: educational achievement, economic opportunity, and social participation. It is the most important because having access (first level) and skills (second level) does not guarantee benefit. Example: Two students both have home internet access and basic digital skills. One uses the internet for Khan Academy, college research, and online AP courses. The other uses it primarily for social media and entertainment. The difference in outcomes is a third-level divide driven by digital literacy, guidance, cultural capital, and awareness of how technology can advance educational goals. Addressing the third level requires not just providing technology but teaching students how to use it productively for learning.

rubric.key: Explains why outcomes matter more than access

questions.question: A U.S.-based NGO wants to deploy an English-language adaptive learning platform in rural Guatemala. Beyond translating the text to Spanish, identify three cultural or contextual adaptations needed for the platform to be effective.
questions.answer: (1) Language: Many rural Guatemalan students speak Mayan languages (K'iche', Kaqchikel, Mam) as their first language, not Spanish. The platform may need indigenous language support or at minimum bilingual Spanish/indigenous scaffolding. (2) Content context: Examples, scenarios, and images designed for U.S. students (suburban settings, American cultural references, individualistic achievement framing) will not resonate. Content must use local contexts: agricultural settings, community values, local currency and measurement systems. (3) Pedagogical culture: Guatemalan classrooms tend to be more teacher-centered and collectivist. A platform designed for American-style self-directed individual learning may feel unfamiliar. Adding collaborative features and teacher-guided modes would better match local educational culture.

rubric.key: Identifies a content/context adaptation beyond translation

questions.question: What is 'pilot-itis' in international ed tech development, and why do pilots that succeed often fail to scale? Propose one strategy to mitigate this problem.
questions.answer: Pilot-itis is the tendency for development organizations to continuously fund small-scale pilots that show promising results but never reach scale. Pilots succeed because they receive disproportionate attention: dedicated project managers, extra training, technical support, and motivated participants. At scale, these resources are spread thin or unavailable. Additionally, the organizational and political challenges of system-wide adoption (institutional resistance, policy changes, infrastructure requirements) do not manifest in small pilots. Mitigation strategy: design pilots as 'minimal viable scale' from the start — implement in a full school district rather than one school, use the district's existing support structures rather than adding project staff, and budget for the full cost of scale rather than subsidized pilot costs. This reveals scalability challenges early rather than after a success narrative has been built.

rubric.key: Explains why pilot success does not predict scale success

major_assignments.title: Global Ed Tech Case Study and Policy Brief
major_assignments.description: Select an educational technology initiative in a low- or middle-income country. Conduct a comprehensive case study including context analysis, implementation description, outcomes evidence, cultural considerations, and sustainability assessment. Then write a policy brief recommending how the initiative could be improved or adapted for a different country context.
objectives: Analyze a real ed tech initiative in a global context
Apply cultural, economic, and infrastructure lenses to evaluation
Write a professional policy brief with actionable recommendations
Consider sustainability and scalability beyond pilot success
deliverables: Case study (3,000 words) with evidence from multiple sources
Policy brief (1,500 words) with specific recommendations
Country context comparison (original country vs. proposed adaptation country)
Sustainability assessment using a structured framework

criteria: Comprehensive context analysis including cultural, economic, and infrastructure factors
Accurate description of the initiative's design and implementation
Professional policy brief format and tone
Cultural dimensions analyzed with Hofstede or similar framework
Respect for local knowledge and practices
Considers what happens when external funding ends
Rigorous case study with multiple evidence sources
Actionable policy brief with feasible recommendations

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on global ed tech landscapes, policy, and cultural analysis

participation.description: Country presentations, case discussions, and cross-cultural dialogue

major_assignments.description: Global ed tech case study and policy brief

midterm_examination.description: Covers digital divide, regional landscapes, and development frameworks

final_project.description: Group project: design an ed tech intervention for a specific LMIC context with budget, timeline, and evaluation plan

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected. Country presentations and cross-cultural discussions require participation. More than 3 absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Country presentations cannot be rescheduled.
course_policies.collaboration: Group discussions and peer feedback are integral. Case studies and policy briefs must be individually authored unless a group project is specified.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citation. Use of AI-generated text must be disclosed. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access. Access to UNESCO and World Bank data portals for research.
